management_notes:
Marathon Gold was led by President and CEO Matt Manson, appointed August 2019. Manson holds a PhD in Geology from the University of Toronto and previously raised C$900M to build Quebec's first diamond mine (Stornoway Diamonds), bringing credible project development credentials. A COO hire (Gil Lawson, 36 years of experience) in September 2023 signalled operational readiness ahead of construction completion.

Insider ownership was a material weakness. Board and management owned less than 1% of the company in aggregate — disclosed value was no more than C$1.8M in a company trading at ~C$200M market cap. The largest disclosed institutional shareholder was BlackRock at 7.1%, with retail public owning approximately 59% of shares. This level of insider alignment is low for a junior developer and is a structural red flag in the Verdict Framework.

The CEO's decision to accept an all-stock deal at C$0.84 per share — widely criticized by investors as representing only ~0.3x unadjusted NAV — reinforced concerns that management prioritized a near-term exit over long-term shareholder value creation. The Calibre transaction was presented as offering construction capital and balance sheet support, but the implied price was deeply discounted to any reasonable NAV estimate.
geology_notes:
Valentine Gold is one of the highest-quality junior gold assets in Atlantic Canada — the largest undeveloped gold resource in Atlantic Canada at time of acquisition. The December 2022 Updated Feasibility Study (FS, the highest study tier) confirmed Proven Mineral Reserves of 1.43 Moz (23.36 Mt at 1.89 g/t Au) and Probable Mineral Reserves of 1.27 Moz (28.22 Mt at 1.40 g/t Au), for total Proven & Probable reserves of 2.70 Moz. Measured Resources (inclusive of reserves) stand at 2.06 Moz and Indicated at 1.90 Moz, with an additional 1.10 Moz Inferred — bringing total M&I+Inferred resources to approximately 5.06 Moz.

The study level is a full Feasibility Study (FS, effective November 30, 2022) — the highest classification, not a PEA or PFS. The 3-pit mine plan covers the Marathon, Leprechaun, and Berry deposits with a 14.3-year mine life and average annual production of ~195,000 oz Au. Grades are solid for an open-pit operation (P&P blend ~1.63 g/t Au). The project sits in Newfoundland and Labrador, an established Tier-1 Canadian mining jurisdiction. Under Calibre Mining post-acquisition, first gold was achieved at Valentine in Q2/Q3 2025.

A 3.0% NSR royalty held by Franco-Nevada (1.5% acquired 2019, additional 1.5% acquired June 2023 for US$45M) reduces project economics but simultaneously validates asset quality — Franco-Nevada does not pay US$45M for marginal royalties. The FS earns a maximum geology score of 5: P&P reserves, full FS, Tier-1 jurisdiction.
capital_notes:
At time of acquisition (November 2023 announcement), Marathon had approximately 393–402 million basic shares outstanding and a fully diluted count of ~472 million (including 11.2M stock options at ~C$2.08 average strike and 68.2M warrants at ~C$1.35 strike). The warrant overhang at C$1.35 was substantially out of the money at the C$0.84 acquisition price, representing a dilutive but underwater structure.

Financing for construction was partially in place: Marathon had secured a US$225M credit facility (January 2023) and the June 2023 Franco-Nevada royalty sale raised US$45M. However, the November 2023 combination with Calibre was in part driven by Marathon's need for additional balance sheet support to complete the ~C$463M cost-to-complete construction of Valentine. Total initial capex ballooned from C$305M (2021 FS) to ~C$534M (including C$71M sunk costs, 2022 FS), a 75% increase that eroded investor confidence. The company was in a capital-constrained position relative to construction requirements, which is what brought it to the table for an at-market acquisition. Score of 2 reflects the bloated share count, construction capex escalation, and capital-constrained negotiating position.
catalyst_notes:
At time of acquisition (January 2024), Valentine Gold was already under active construction — construction commenced October 2022. Under Calibre, construction reached 64% complete by May 2024 with engineering 98% complete, and first gold production was targeted for Q2 2025. As of early 2025, Calibre guided Q2 2025 for first gold with steady-state production of ~200,000 oz/year, making this a near-term production asset at time of the MOZ transaction.

For the purposes of this pre-acquisition historical scorecard: the catalyst was construction completion and first gold pour, which was expected within approximately 12–18 months of acquisition close. This is a strong near-term catalyst. The primary risk was a capital shortfall (which the Calibre deal resolved) and any further capex escalation. The construction decision had already been made, permitting was received, and the project was de-risked at the feasibility level.
acquisition_notes:
The acquisition price was C$0.84 per share (all-stock, 0.6164 Calibre shares per MOZ share), implying a total equity value of C$345M fully diluted. The December 2022 Updated FS reported an after-tax NPV(5%) of C$648M at US$1,700/oz gold. With ~472M fully diluted shares, this implies an FS NAV of approximately C$1.37 per diluted share. Per the framework, FS NAV is taken at face value (no discount).

The acquisition at C$0.84 therefore represented approximately 0.61x FS NAV — a deeply discounted exit by any measure. At spot gold prices in 2024 (~US$2,300/oz), an updated FS NAV would be substantially higher (potentially C$2.50+ per share), making the acquisition even more discounted. Retail investors and commentary noted the deal represented only ~0.3x unadjusted NAV. The analyst consensus pre-acquisition target price was C$1.93. Management accepted this distressed valuation due to capital constraints and the C$534M construction capex commitment. Score of 2: the stock was already at a steep discount to FS NAV before the acquisition and the deal crystallized that discount permanently for shareholders.
analyst_summary:
NOTE: MOZ (Marathon Gold Corporation) was delisted from the TSX on January 24, 2024, following a completed all-stock acquisition by Calibre Mining (TSX: CXB) at C$0.84 per share (0.6164 Calibre shares per MOZ share), for a total equity consideration of C$345M fully diluted. The TSX ticker MOZ is no longer active. Any current exposure to the Valentine Gold asset is through Calibre Mining (CXB), which has since merged with Equinox Gold. This scorecard is a retrospective/archival assessment. Confidence: low.

As a historical scorecard: Valentine Gold was an exceptional geological asset — a full Feasibility Study (FS, December 2022) with 2.70 Moz Proven & Probable reserves (14.3-year mine life, ~195,000 oz/yr average production, FS study level) in Tier-1 Newfoundland. The after-tax NPV(5%) was C$648M at US$1,700/oz gold, implying FS NAV per diluted share of approximately C$1.37. The acquisition at C$0.84 (P/NAV ~0.61x) was a poor outcome for MOZ retail shareholders, driven by capital constraints, a bloated ~472M diluted share count with underwater warrants, sub-1% insider ownership that reduced alignment incentives, and construction capex escalation from C$305M to C$534M.

The composite score of 15/25 (WATCH) reflects the mixed picture: world-class geology and a de-risked FS project in a safe jurisdiction (would justify BUY on geology alone) versus weak management alignment, capital structure strain, and a distressed sale that destroyed significant shareholder value relative to FS NAV. First gold at Valentine was achieved under Calibre in Q2/Q3 2025.
